title: Aosagi
description: Adachi wakes up in Shimamura's body. A body-swap story from the other side—navigating Shimamura's family, her mother's teasing, and the ache of being separated from the person she loves most.
content:
When I woke up that morning, something felt wrong.

It was subtle at first—like waking under a sunrise I'd never seen before—yet even the blink of my eyes told me something was off. Confusion grew as I pushed myself upright, and the strange sensation swelled with it.

The sight before me looked familiar… but it had absolutely no connection to last night.

I wasn't in my own bed. I was lying on a futon spread across the floor.

Bracing myself with both hands, I slowly looked around the room.

This room. This smell.

'Shimamura…'

The moment I murmured her name, a cold shiver bolted up my spine, and I instinctively jumped back.

That was Shimamura's voice. And it had come out of my mouth.

Panic scrambled my limbs, and I slipped helplessly on the wooden floor as if I were skidding across ice. Desperate, I crawled toward the window, yanked open the curtains, and stared at the reflection in the glass.

My eyes widened.

'…I turned into Shimamura.'

Was it because I thought about her so much? Had I finally reached the point where I literally became her?

The sunlight was painfully bright, but I barely noticed it. Sweat streamed down my forehead as I clutched my head and groaned.

I had become Shimamura.

Should I be happy? Should I be devastated? Once the shock faded away, I wasn't sure what emotions were supposed to remain.

'Wh-What do I do… what do I do…?'

I spun in place with both hands still half-raised. Moving didn't feel right—like my limbs were attached with loose strings. Every step came with strange little shivers running from my heart to my fingertips.

I couldn't accept that I was controlling Shimamura's body so poorly, so I forced myself to stop, breathe, and flex each finger one by one. I focused carefully—matching my thoughts to each motion until the dissonance eased.

I am Shimamura.

I repeated this a few times until my mind settled.

Only then did I step out of the room and head toward the noise downstairs. Maybe… maybe Shimamura herself would be waiting there. A tiny, impossible hope fluttered in my chest.

'Oh, you're finally up,' Shimamura's mother said as soon as I entered the kitchen.

So this really was Shimamura's house.

She squinted at me, and I flinched.

'G-Good morn—'

'Who are you?!'

That line hit with the force of a punch to the gut. I choked on my own breath.

'Everyone, come quick! There's a suspicious person here!!'

'W-Wait! I'm not—'

My protest vanished under her shouting. And despite all that yelling… no one came.

'Hm. You're pretty unpopular, huh~' she nodded with strange satisfaction.

Shimamura's little sister wandered by with her schoolbag.

'I'm off!'

'Okay, be careful! Everyone's gone now~!'

And just like that, Shimamura's mother leaned in toward me again.

'Good morning~?'

Her face was far too close. Suspicious eyes bore into mine.

I couldn't pretend to be Shimamura. I was already exposed.

The thought nearly escaped me.

'G-Good morning…'

'Hougetsu usually calls me 'Mother Dearest,' doesn't she?'

I almost answered instinctively—my body reacting before my brain—but I caught myself.

'Wh-Who are you supposed to be?'

I tried to imitate Shimamura's manner as best as I could. Shimamura's mother laughed.

'Yes, yes—that's the tone.'

She patted my shoulder, satisfied, as if she'd just taught me how to pretend to be her daughter.

'Now hurry and eat breakfast so I can clean up.'

'Ah—y-yes…'

I sat down at her seat, where a bowl of rice and thick slices of tamagoyaki waited.

Right. Shimamura loves tamagoyaki.

'Which furikake do you want?' She shook three colorful packets at me like prizes.

'Eh, um… anything's fine…'

'Hmm~? Don't you always choose the nori-egg one?'

'O-Of course. Yes. Nori-egg.'

Too careless. I'd never once talked with Shimamura about furikake.

Her mother handed it to me while tilting her head playfully.

'But you don't usually use furikake at all.'

'…What?'

So she had figured me out and was teasing me. Shimamura's mother was terrifying.

She sat across from me, resting her chin in her hands, staring openly at my every move. Her gaze had no filter—like sunlight piercing straight through a window.

'Oh? Since when did you become left-handed?'

'Ah.'

I froze—rice bowl in my right hand, chopsticks in my left.

I quickly switched, nearly dropping everything. Her expression didn't change at all. Which only made her silence scarier.

I forced myself to eat while avoiding her eyes. Moving this unfamiliar body felt uncomfortable; every gesture reminded me that my thoughts and her physical habits didn't align.

I wasn't doing a good job pretending to be Shimamura. I wasn't convincing at all.

But today was a school day. I had no choice.

I needed to meet… myself.

I finished breakfast at a pace terrible for digestion, murmured a shaky 'Thanks,' and stood.

'There,' she said, pointing. 'Your lunch.'

'Ah—thank…'

'Who are you?!'

I nearly burst into tears.

Drained from the morning alone, I escaped to the washroom. I knew this house well enough—I'd stayed here plenty of times.

But the moment I saw Shimamura's face in the mirror, dissatisfaction punched through me.

Touching my cheek didn't help.

She was beautiful—objectively so—but it wasn't mine. The gentle sunlight that usually rested on Shimamura's shoulders… wasn't there.

My own smile looked wrong on her face—cheap, tasteless, like I was defiling her. Disgusted with the imposter in the mirror, I shut my eyes and brushed my teeth.

Cold water helped cool my flushed face.

Then—time to change into my uniform.

Change clothes… Change clothes.

A dreadful realization struck me like lightning. Shimamura wasn't wearing anything under her pajamas—or rather, she was, but only on the bottom. On top… there was nothing.

'..............................................................................'

I could almost hear tiny blood vessels bursting in my ears.

All my calm evaporated.

I fought the urge to look down—her chest, my chest, no, her chest—and twisted my head away. Memories of the time she caught me sneaking a peek flashed through my mind, and I wanted to ram my head against the wall.

But I couldn't. This was Shimamura's body.

And that thought alone was enough to mentally destroy me.

Muttering nonsense to stabilize my sanity, I changed with my eyes mostly closed. It was impractical and made everything harder, but opening them would've been… fatal.

Even fully dressed, every accidental movement made my face flare. The sensation of simply existing in her skin was overwhelming.

I had forgotten how precious Shimamura's body was—to me.

I can't do this. I have to run.

I grabbed the lunchbox and bolted out the door, sprinting toward my house.

When I finally arrived—Seeing my own body walk out of my home was surreal.

We stared straight at each other.

'Um… who might you be?'

'Eh? I'm Adachi.'

'………………………………'

She lied without hesitation—just to mess with me. Definitely Shimamura.

We rode her bike to school together. During class, I couldn't stop staring at my own back. Just sitting upright made me look like a different person altogether.

At lunch, I approached… myself.

'Shimamura, your mother packed this for you.'

'That's not the one I received.'

She gently pushed it back toward me.

So I had to eat it after all.

'Shimamura's mother—' I began.

'She's annoying, right?'

'N-No… she's just… energetic.'

'Your mom got scared. …Well, I scared her.'

'What did you do?'

'I cheerfully greeted her.'

'…Yeah. That would terrify her.'

Shimamura had commented earlier on eating breakfast properly. This experience was probably why. I thought of one thing I wanted to tell her.

'Shimamura,' I said.

'Hm?'

'When you… get a lunchbox from your mom… you should say thank you.'

Because getting questioned over it nearly killed me. She paused, then deliberately lowered her head like a scolded child.

'Okaaay~'

Seeing my own face soften like that made my skin crawl.

After school, she declared confidently:

'We'll switch back tomorrow.'

'What if… we don't?'

'Then we'll just do our best to live!'

Words I would never say—but from her, somehow reassuring.

I went home, exhausted.

'Welcome back, sis~' Shimamura's little sister greeted me while washing something in the yard.

'Ah—yes. I'm home.'

From now until tomorrow… I had to deal with her. A formidable opponent.

'Have you seen Yashiro?'

'Yashiro?'

'I was gonna take a bath with her too.'

She smiled innocently. She always acted harsher toward me, but so naturally around Shimamura.

Just like… me.

Inside, I peeked toward the kitchen.

'I'm home…' I whispered.

'Here's the lunchbox,' I added.

'Alrighty~' Her mother took it with a wide grin. 'Was it yummy~?'

Another trap. Every answer felt wrong. Every response earned another 'Who are you?!'

I was failing at being Shimamura. After barely surviving, I fled to her room and sat against the wall, still in uniform. Alone at last, I exhaled.

At home, it was always quiet. I rarely spoke.

Tonight until tomorrow felt unbearably long. Dusk lingered in my thoughts like it would never fade. I didn't like noisy places. But maybe Shimamura would find my house too lonely.

I didn't mind waiting quietly for her, like watching a dark ocean from the beach—knowing the sun would eventually rise. Even loneliness could be beautiful if you knew it wouldn't last forever.

But if I stayed like this… The sun would never rise.

As long as I remained Shimamura… I could never see Shimamura.

That was a misery I couldn't endure. So I rested my head against the wall, knees to my chest.

And with a tiny prayer—like singing myself to sleep—I whispered:

'Please… let me see Shimamura tomorrow.'

The next morning, I really did wake up in my own bed.

I rushed to the mirror, checking every angle to make sure I'd turned back to normal. Only after confirming it did I let out a long, complicated sigh.

'…Shimamura.'

Her name slipped out before I could stop it, and I hugged it close—pointless, but comforting.

Then I remembered what she told me yesterday and decided to eat breakfast properly, so I headed downstairs toward the kitchen.

Part of having breakfast was… at least sharing a moment with my mother in the morning. Maybe that's what Shimamura had in mind. Or maybe she was just thinking about my health. Either way, it could be both.

And then—the moment I stepped off the last stair, I heard it.

A kitchen knife rhythmically hitting the cutting board, loud and energetic—way too energetic. The noisy, almost drum-like chopping felt completely out of place in our usually quiet home, so I leaned over to peek into the kitchen.

'Good morning~!'

My mother—my mother?—noticed me immediately and cheerfully waved.

'Eh…? Ehh?'

She set down the knife and ran over to me with a bright, sparkling smile that sent chills crawling down my spine.

'Oh~ my sweet—uh, how do you say it again—daugh~ter~!'

Then, as if she were in some kind of musical, she grabbed me and spun me around. This was absolutely—Absolutely someone else.

'W–Wait… are you—Shimamura's… mother?'

'Who even is that~?'

Yep. That confirmed it.

I asked again. And again. And again—ten times at least. But every single time, she blocked me with the exact same line—'Who even is that?'—like some kind of verbal martial-arts counter technique.

And so, in what barely counted as a conversation, I was forced to listen while my 'mother' talked nonstop.

She never even gave me time to respond. She just kept chattering away, all while pushing an absurdly huge breakfast in front of me.

'T–This is too much…'

'Eat more, eat more~!'

After that, she handed me two lunch boxes—two!—and smacked my back with enough force to rattle my bones before sending me out the door with overwhelming cheer.

'Uh—so—what was your name again? Sorry, I hit my head like five times and forgot.'

'Um… I'm Sakura, Shimamura's mother.'

'Who even is that?! Take care on your way, Sakura! Get along with Hougetsu, okay?'

'…Y-Yes… I will.'

The way my mother—no, the woman wearing my mother's face—beamed as she saw me off… I had a feeling I would never witness that again.

My back, still warm from her smacks, tingled with heat.

Really. A lot.
translatorNote: Akakura and Aosagi — These chapter titles reference Mount Akakura (赤倉山, Akakura-yama) and Aosagi Island (青鷺島, Aosagi-jima), both locations from Remember 11: The Age of Infinity. Mount Akakura is the snowy mountain, and Aosagi Island (literally 'Blue Heron Island') is the fictional island where the VN's events take place.
